subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Composition of Federal Spending, Fiscal Years 1962–2019
stimulus: Source: Tax Policy Center, 2020. Line graph showing Composition of Federal Spending for Fiscal Years 1962–2019, displaying trends in mandatory spending, discretionary spending, and net interest as percentages of the total federal budget.

parts.prompt: Identify the type of spending that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.
parts.scoring_notes: Only mandatory spending earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the type of spending that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.
wrong_examples: Discretionary spending. (Incorrect — by 2016 mandatory spending had surpassed discretionary spending as the largest budget category)
Defense spending. (Defense is a subcategory of discretionary spending — not one of the three categories shown in the graph)
common_traps: Students who recall that discretionary spending historically dominated the budget may incorrectly choose it; by 2016, mandatory spending (Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid) had grown to be the largest category
The graph shows three categories — mandatory, discretionary, and net interest — make sure to identify one of these three, not a subcategory
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the type of spending that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016. Acceptable identification: Mandatory spending.

sample_responses.response: Mandatory spending comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies mandatory spending as the largest budget category in 2016 based on the graph. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Discretionary spending was the largest share of the federal budget in 2016.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect — by 2016, mandatory spending had surpassed discretionary spending as the largest share of the federal budget. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Describe the difference in the trends in mandatory versus discretionary spending as illustrated in the graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must describe both trends (mandatory increasing, discretionary decreasing) as a contrasting pattern. Describing only one trend does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Describe the difference in the trends in mandatory versus discretionary spending as illustrated in the graph.

required_elements: As a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased whereas during that same time frame discretionary spending decreased.
alternatives.correct_example: As a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased whereas during that same time frame discretionary spending decreased.

wrong_examples: Mandatory spending is higher than discretionary spending. (Describes the current state, not the contrasting trends over time)
Federal spending increased over time. (Describes overall spending, not the contrasting trends between the two specific categories)
common_traps: The question asks about BOTH trends in contrast — a response that describes only mandatory spending or only discretionary spending does not fully answer the question
Be precise about direction: mandatory spending increased as a share of the budget; discretionary spending decreased as a share — both directions must be stated to earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the difference in the trends in mandatory versus discretionary spending. Acceptable description: As a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased whereas during that same time frame discretionary spending decreased.

sample_responses.response: As a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased over time from 1962 to 2019, while discretionary spending decreased over that same period.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly contrasts both trends — mandatory spending increasing and discretionary spending decreasing as shares of the total federal budget. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Mandatory spending has been higher than discretionary spending in recent years.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a snapshot (current relative levels), not the contrasting trends over time. Must state both directions of change to earn the point. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about Congress's budgetary choices based on trends in the data.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion (an inference about what Congress is choosing to do), not just describe the data. Accept any plausible conclusion about Congressional budgetary behavior supported by the trends.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about Congress's budgetary choices based on trends in the data.

required_elements: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress decreases discretionary spending.
alternatives.correct_example: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress decreases discretionary spending.

required_elements: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress raises taxes.
alternatives.correct_example: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress raises taxes.

required_elements: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress continues similar funding levels and increases the budget deficit.
alternatives.correct_example: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress continues similar funding levels and increases the budget deficit.

wrong_examples: Mandatory spending has gone up and discretionary spending has gone down. (Describes the data — does not draw a conclusion about what this means for Congressional decision-making)
Congress spends too much money. (Evaluative statement not supported by a specific data trend — does not draw a conclusion based on the graph)
common_traps: A conclusion must explain what Congress IS doing or HAS DONE in response to the trends — not just restate what the data shows

Acceptable conclusions include: Congress cuts discretionary spending to offset rising mandatory spending, Congress borrows more (increases deficit), or Congress raises taxes; all must be connected to the trend in the graph
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for drawing a conclusion about Congress's budgetary choices based on trends in the data. Acceptable conclusions include: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress decreases discretionary spending. Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress raises taxes. Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress continues similar funding levels and increases the budget deficit.

sample_responses.response: Because mandatory spending has increased as a share of the federal budget over time, Congress has responded by allowing discretionary spending to decrease as a proportion of total spending in order to partially offset the growth in mandatory program costs.
sample_responses.commentary: Goes beyond describing the data to draw a conclusion about Congress's budgetary behavior — Congress is squeezing discretionary spending in response to rising mandatory obligations. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Mandatory spending has increased from about 30% to over 60% of the federal budget since 1962.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a data point rather than drawing a conclusion about Congressional budgetary choices. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how an interest group might respond to trends shown in the graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific response connected to the spending trends — not just state that interest groups respond to spending. Accept any plausible and specific interest group response.

scoring_points.description: Explain how an interest group might respond to trends shown in the graph.

required_elements: As discretionary spending decreases, interest groups might be more motivated to fight for the survival of policies they support.
alternatives.correct_example: As discretionary spending decreases, interest groups might be more motivated to fight for the survival of policies they support.

required_elements: Interest groups might lobby for changes to programs funded by mandatory spending that would benefit their members.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups might lobby for changes to programs funded by mandatory spending that would benefit their members.

required_elements: Interest groups may lobby more aggressively for programs funded by discretionary spending since discretionary spending is decreasing as a percentage of the total budget.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups may lobby more aggressively for programs funded by discretionary spending since discretionary spending is decreasing as a percentage of the total budget.

wrong_examples: Interest groups would try to get more money. (Does not connect to the specific data trends or name a specific lobbying mechanism)
Interest groups would protest the spending cuts. (Too general — must explain specifically how the trend in the chart motivates a particular interest group strategy)
common_traps: The interest group response must be tied to a specific trend in the graph — e.g., because discretionary spending is shrinking, groups dependent on discretionary programs face greater competition and must lobby more aggressively
Accept responses about groups on either side: groups protecting mandatory programs (Social Security, Medicare) from cuts, or groups seeking more discretionary funding for their programs
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how an interest group might respond to trends shown in the graph. Acceptable explanations include: As discretionary spending decreases, interest groups might be more motivated to fight for the survival of policies they support. Interest groups might lobby for changes to programs funded by mandatory spending that would benefit their members. Interest groups may lobby more aggressively for programs funded by discretionary spending since discretionary spending is decreasing as a percentage of the total budget.

sample_responses.response: As discretionary spending decreases as a share of the federal budget, interest groups that depend on discretionary programs — such as education advocacy groups or defense contractors — would likely lobby Congress more aggressively to protect funding for their programs, since there is an increasingly smaller share of the budget available for discretionary allocation.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the specific data trend (declining discretionary spending) to a specific interest group response (more aggressive lobbying to protect discretionary program funding). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Interest groups would try to influence Congress's spending decisions.
sample_responses.commentary: Too general — does not explain how the specific trends in the graph would motivate a particular kind of interest group response. 0 pts.
